symbols: rising waters — God's judgment on a corrupt world
people drowning — the consequence of wickedness and rejection of God
description: A dramatic painting by Hans Baldung showing the Great Flood. People and animals struggle in rising waters, some climbing trees or rocks, others drowning. The scene is chaotic and terrifying, depicting the overwhelming destruction described in Genesis.
theological_description: The Great Flood (GEN.6-8) is God's judgment on a world filled with violence and corruption: 'The LORD saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every intention of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually' (GEN.6.5). Only Noah, 'a righteous man, blameless in his generation' (GEN.6.9), found grace. The Flood is both judgment and salvation — destruction of the wicked and preservation of the faithful. Peter draws the typological connection to baptism: 'God's patience waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was being prepared, in which a few, that is, eight persons, were brought safely through water. Baptism, which corresponds to this, now saves you' (1PE.3.20-21). Jesus warned that his return would be like the days of Noah (MAT.24.37-39).

scripture_refs.reason: God seeing the wickedness that provoked the Flood
The waters rising and covering the earth
The Flood as a type of baptism
As in the days of Noah, so will be the coming of the Son of Man
